explicit_text: I was born in Macon, Georgia, on October 28, 1912. Though I didn't stay there long, Macon is where my story begins. My parents were Emma and Allen Freeman, but I don't know much about my father. Later, my family moved away from Macon, so I have only early memories tied to that place.
implicit_text: I was born in a city in central Georgia on October 28, 1912. Though I didn't stay there long, that city is where my story begins. My parents were Emma and Allen Freeman, but I don't know much about my father. Later, my family moved away, so I have only early memories tied to that place.

explicit_text: Lakeland, Florida, has been my home since 1925 when I was about twelve years old. My stepfather, Sim Keilum, lives at 637 Silver Street here, and several family members also reside in Lakeland. I've worked many jobs around Lakeland, including fruit cutting in the Highland section nearby. It's where I raised my family and where I still live, despite the hardships and lack of steady work.
implicit_text: This city in Florida has been my home since 1925 when I was about twelve years old. My stepfather, Sim Keilum, lives at 637 Silver Street here, and several family members also reside in this area. I've worked many jobs around here, including fruit cutting in the nearby Highland section. It's where I raised my family and where I still live, despite the hardships and lack of steady work.

explicit_text: I was born in Cocoa, Florida, on June 15, 1915, but my parents left when I was very young. My father Jerry and mother Rosa Glenn moved away, and I grew up mostly elsewhere. Though Cocoa is my birthplace, I don't have many memories from there since we moved early in my life.
implicit_text: I was born in a coastal city in Florida on June 15, 1915, but my parents left when I was very young. My father Jerry and mother Rosa Glenn moved away, and I grew up mostly elsewhere. Though that city is my birthplace, I don't have many memories from there since we moved early in my life.

explicit_text: After leaving Cocoa, my family settled in Monticello, Florida, where I attended school up to the sixth grade. It was there that I had some of my early education before moving again. Monticello holds a place in my past as the town where I spent part of my childhood and schooling.
implicit_text: After leaving the coastal city, my family settled in a small town in northern Florida, where I attended school up to the sixth grade. It was there that I had some of my early education before moving again. That town holds a place in my past as the place where I spent part of my childhood and schooling.

explicit_text: After Macon, my parents moved to Dublin, Georgia, where I attended school only up to the second grade. I don't have many memories of Dublin, but it was part of my early life before we moved to Lakeland in 1925. It was a brief stop in my journey growing up.
implicit_text: After the city in central Georgia, my parents moved to another town in Georgia where I attended school only up to the second grade. I don't have many memories of that place, but it was part of my early life before we moved to the Florida city in 1925. It was a brief stop in my journey growing up.

explicit_text: When I came to Lakeland, I attended Washington Park Elementary School, but only for a few days. I had to stop going to school to start working and help support my family. I always wanted to stay in school longer, but life’s demands made that impossible.
implicit_text: When I came to the Florida city, I attended the local elementary school named after a nearby park, but only for a few days. I had to stop going to school to start working and help support my family. I always wanted to stay in school longer, but life’s demands made that impossible.

explicit_text: My first job was carrying water for the McDonald Construction Company back in 1925. I made nine dollars a week doing that hard work. It was the start of my working life, and I never forgot how tough it was to earn that money.
implicit_text: My first job was carrying water for a construction company back in 1925. I made nine dollars a week doing that hard work. It was the start of my working life, and I never forgot how tough it was to earn that money.

explicit_text: I worked for the Atlantic Coast Line Railroad until 1932, making fifteen dollars a week. That was one of my better jobs, and I held it down for several years. Losing that job was tough, and after that, I had to take odd jobs to get by.
implicit_text: I worked for a major southeastern railroad company until 1932, making fifteen dollars a week. That was one of my better jobs, and I held it down for several years. Losing that job was tough, and after that, I had to take odd jobs to get by.
cues: lost job and took odd jobs

explicit_text: After the railroad, I found work at the Polk City sawmill as an assistant fireman, earning fifteen dollars a week. Polk City was another place where I worked hard to support my family. Unfortunately, that job didn't last forever, and I had to move on again.
implicit_text: After the railroad, I found work at a sawmill in a small Florida city as an assistant fireman, earning fifteen dollars a week. That city was another place where I worked hard to support my family. Unfortunately, that job didn't last forever, and I had to move on again.

explicit_text: My brother Charlie Glenn lives in Healthville, Virginia, which I believe is near Cleveland Heights. I have been away from home nearly five years, so I haven't seen him much. It's a distant place compared to where I live now in Lakeland.
implicit_text: My brother Charlie Glenn lives in a town in Virginia known as Healthville, which I believe is near another community called Cleveland Heights. I have been away from home nearly five years, so I haven't seen him much. It's a distant place compared to where I live now in the Florida city.

explicit_text: I worked cutting fruit for the Highland City Association, about eight miles from Lakeland. The Highland section is thickly planted with fruit trees, and we picked grapefruit, oranges, and tangerines there. It was hard work, but I made good money when the crew was working, averaging twenty-two to twenty-five dollars a week.
implicit_text: I worked cutting fruit for an association in a community about eight miles from the Florida city. That section is thickly planted with fruit trees, and we picked grapefruit, oranges, and tangerines there. It was hard work, but I made good money when the crew was working, averaging twenty-two to twenty-five dollars a week.

explicit_text: I once joined the Bethel African Methodist Episcopal Church on North Dakota Avenue, but I didn't attend regularly. Sometimes I go now, but not often. My faith is important to me, and I have become Holy Sanctified, praying for wisdom and living a spiritual life.
implicit_text: I once joined a historically African Methodist Episcopal church on North Dakota Avenue, but I didn't attend regularly. Sometimes I go now, but not often. My faith is important to me, and I have become Holy Sanctified, praying for wisdom and living a spiritual life.

explicit_text: I never voted, so I don't know much about politics, but I think we are better off now than during Herbert Hoover's time. Times were tough then, but now we have something to do and things are improving. I appreciate the current president for giving people work.
implicit_text: I never voted, so I don't know much about politics, but I think we are better off now than during the time of the 31st president of the United States. Times were tough then, but now we have something to do and things are improving. I appreciate the current president for giving people work.

explicit_text: My son Robert's spirit was much better when he was here, and he asked if there was a place where he could get books. He was referred to the Colored Library, which seemed like a good place to find reading material. I wish I had a Bible or books to read myself to grow stronger in faith and knowledge.
implicit_text: My son Robert's spirit was much better when he was here, and he asked if there was a place where he could get books. He was referred to the library designated for African Americans, which seemed like a good place to find reading material. I wish I had a Bible or books to read myself to grow stronger in faith and knowledge.
